mnemonics.title: One Way Street
mnemonics.content: Singly linked lists only move forward, like a one-way street; each node points only to the next node.

mnemonics.title: Two-Way Street
mnemonics.content: Doubly linked lists have pointers to both next and previous nodes, like a two-way street where you can go back and forth.

mnemonics.title: Merry-Go-Round
mnemonics.content: In a circular linked list, the last node points back to the first node, forming a loop like a merry-go-round.

mnemonics.title: Node as a Wagon
mnemonics.content: Each element (node) is like a wagon that carries data and a hook (pointer) to the next wagon.

mnemonics.title: HEAD-TAIL Train
mnemonics.content: HEAD is the first node (engine), TAIL is the last node (caboose). Used to know start and end of the list.

mnemonics.title: Walk the Track
mnemonics.content: Traversing a linked list means walking along the track, visiting each node in order until you reach the end (NULL).

acronyms.usage: When you see SLL, think of a linear chain with only next pointers.
When you see DLL, recall that each node has both next and prev pointers.
When you see CLL, remember the last node's next points to head, forming a circle.
N stands for Node; a node is the building block containing data and pointer(s).
HEAD points to the first node; used to start traversal.
TAIL points to the last node; useful for O(1) append operations.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a row of boxes (nodes) each with an arrow pointing to the next box; the last box points to NULL (empty).
Draw boxes with two arrows: one arrow points right to the next node, another arrow points left to the previous node.
Draw a ring of boxes where the arrow from the last box loops back to the first box.
Picture a node as a small envelope: the front holds the data (letter), the back has a slot for the address of the next envelope.
Imagine walking along a path, stepping from one node to the next, like following a chain of signposts.

analogies.analogy: A train of single-car wagons where each wagon is hitched only to the one in front.
A double-ended hallway where you can walk forward and backward, each door connects to the previous and next room.
A carousel or merry-go-round where the ride loops continuously.
A person holding a rope that is tied to the next person, forming a human chain.
A GPS coordinate that tells you where the next location is.
